summary:
The New Zealand Wars (1845–72), also known as the Land Wars, were a series of armed conflicts between the British colonial government and Maori iwi (tribes) over land, sovereignty, and the terms of the Treaty of Waitangi (1840). They were among the most sustained and militarily sophisticated colonial conflicts in the Pacific and resulted in the confiscation of millions of acres of Maori land, reshaping the demographic and economic landscape of the North Island.

The wars unfolded in multiple phases across different regions. The Northern War (1845–46), the Hutt Valley and Whanganui campaigns of the late 1840s, and the Taranaki and Waikato Wars of the 1860s were the most significant engagements. The Waikato War of 1863–64, fought against the Maori King Movement (Kingitanga) which had sought to create a unified Maori political institution capable of negotiating on equal terms, brought the largest British military force deployed in New Zealand. Maori fighters, often operating from elaborately constructed pa (fortified villages) designed to neutralize British artillery, demonstrated remarkable tactical innovation.

The confiscations (raupatu) that followed defeat were politically consequential far beyond the military outcomes. Large tracts of fertile North Island land in Taranaki, Waikato, and the Bay of Plenty were seized under the New Zealand Settlements Act (1863) and distributed to British settlers. These confiscations remained a source of grievance through the twentieth century and were the subject of Treaty settlements negotiated from the 1990s onward. The wars effectively broke Maori economic and political independence in the North Island, though Maori culture and population survived and eventually revived.
